map.condition: Checking if a relation is an equivalence (reflexive + symmetric + transitive)
map.use: \forall x\,R(x,x)\;\land\;\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))\;\land\;\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\land R(y,z)\to R(x,z))

formulas.name: Universal Quantifier
formulas.latex: \forall x\, P(x) \equiv P(a_1) \land P(a_2) \land \cdots \land P(a_n) \text{ (finite domain)}
formulas.plain: For all x, P(x) — true when P holds for every element in the domain
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'every', 'all', 'any', 'each' into predicate logic

terms.means: for every x in the domain

formulas.trap: Universal statements over an empty domain are vacuously TRUE; don't assume the domain is non-empty.

formulas.name: Existential Quantifier
formulas.latex: \exists x\, P(x) \equiv P(a_1) \lor P(a_2) \lor \cdots \lor P(a_n) \text{ (finite domain)}
formulas.plain: There exists x such that P(x) — true when P holds for at least one element
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'some', 'there exists', 'at least one' into predicate logic

terms.means: there is at least one x in the domain

formulas.trap: Existential statements over an empty domain are FALSE; unlike universal which is vacuously true.

formulas.name: Unique Existential Quantifier
formulas.latex: \exists!\, x\, P(x) \equiv \exists x\,(P(x) \land \forall y\,(P(y) \to y = x))
formulas.plain: There exists exactly one x such that P(x)
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing 'exactly one' or 'there is a unique element' satisfying a predicate

terms.means: identity; any other element satisfying P must be x itself

formulas.trap: \exists! is shorthand — always expand it using \exists + \forall when doing formal proofs or checking satisfiability.

formulas.name: Scope and Free vs. Bound Variables
formulas.latex: \forall x\,P(x,y) \;\text{: } x \text{ is bound}, \; y \text{ is free}
formulas.plain: A variable is bound if it falls within the scope of a quantifier; otherwise it is free
formulas.whenToUse: Determining which variables are free or bound before substitution or renaming

terms.means: variable governed by a quantifier in scope
variable not governed by any enclosing quantifier

formulas.trap: A variable can be both free (one occurrence) and bound (another occurrence) in the same formula — treat each occurrence independently.

groups.groupTitle: Negation of Quantifiers (De Morgan for FOL)

formulas.name: Negation of Universal Quantifier
formulas.plain: NOT (for all x, P(x))  =  there exists x such that NOT P(x)
formulas.whenToUse: Pushing negation inward past a universal quantifier (De Morgan for FOL)
formulas.trap: Students forget to flip the quantifier — they write \forall x\,\neg P(x) instead of \exists x\,\neg P(x).

formulas.name: Negation of Existential Quantifier
formulas.plain: NOT (there exists x such that P(x))  =  for all x, NOT P(x)
formulas.whenToUse: Pushing negation inward past an existential quantifier
formulas.trap: Confusing with propositional De Morgan — the quantifier type flips, the predicate gets negated.

formulas.name: Double Negation of Quantifiers
formulas.plain: Double negation cancels — same as propositional double negation
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying nested negations in FOL formulas
formulas.trap: Only valid in classical logic; be careful if context involves intuitionistic logic.

formulas.name: Distributing \forall over \land
formulas.plain: For all x (P(x) AND Q(x))  is equivalent to  (for all x P(x)) AND (for all x Q(x))
formulas.whenToUse: Splitting or merging universal statements joined by conjunction
formulas.trap: This distribution does NOT hold for \lor — \forall x(P(x)\lor Q(x)) does NOT split into \forall x P(x) \lor \forall x Q(x).

formulas.name: Distributing \exists over \lor
formulas.plain: There exists x (P(x) OR Q(x))  is equivalent to  (exists x P(x)) OR (exists x Q(x))
formulas.whenToUse: Splitting or merging existential statements joined by disjunction
formulas.trap: This does NOT hold for \land — \exists x(P(x)\land Q(x)) does NOT imply \exists x P(x)\land \exists x Q(x).

formulas.name: Order of Quantifiers (Non-Commutativity of Mixed Quantifiers)
formulas.plain: For all x there exists y P(x,y)  is NOT the same as  there exists y for all x P(x,y)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining truth when two different quantifiers appear — order matters!

terms.means: for each x, a (possibly different) y can be chosen
one fixed y works for all x simultaneously

formulas.trap: \exists y\,\forall x implies \forall x\,\exists y, but NOT vice versa — the existential-first version is stronger.

formulas.name: Order of Same Quantifiers (Commutative)
formulas.latex: \forall x\,\forall y\,P(x,y) \equiv \forall y\,\forall x\,P(x,y) \qquad \exists x\,\exists y\,P(x,y) \equiv \exists y\,\exists x\,P(x,y)
formulas.plain: Two universal (or two existential) quantifiers can be swapped without changing meaning
formulas.whenToUse: Rewriting formulas with two consecutive same-type quantifiers for clarity
formulas.trap: Only same-type quantifiers commute — never swap \forall with \exists.

groups.groupTitle: Natural Language to FOL Translation Patterns

formulas.name: Universal Statement with Implication ('Every A is B')
formulas.plain: For all x, if A(x) then B(x) — standard form for 'every A is a B'
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'every', 'all', 'each' followed by an attribute or condition
formulas.trap: Using \land instead of \to — \forall x(A(x)\land B(x)) means 'everything is BOTH A and B', which is much stronger.

formulas.name: Existential Statement with Conjunction ('Some A is B')
formulas.plain: There exists x such that x is A AND x is B — standard form for 'some A is B'
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'some', 'there exists', 'at least one' combined with a property
formulas.trap: Using \to instead of \land — \exists x(A(x)\to B(x)) is almost always true (take any non-A element) and does NOT capture 'some A is B'.

formulas.name: Relational Universal: 'Every mother loves her children'
formulas.plain: For all x, if x is a mother, then for all y, if y is a child of x then x loves y
formulas.whenToUse: Translating possessive/relational universals involving two entities and a binary predicate

terms.means: y is a child of x

formulas.trap: Swapping the order of quantifiers or using \land for 'is a mother' — must use \to for the outer universal.

formulas.name: Negative Universal: 'No A is B'
formulas.plain: No A is B — equivalent to: there is no x that is both A and B
formulas.whenToUse: Translating 'no', 'none of', 'never' in the context of categorical statements
formulas.trap: Students write \forall x(A(x)\land\neg B(x)) — that means 'everything is A but not B', not 'no A is B'.

formulas.name: Satisfiability (SAT)
formulas.plain: A FOL formula is satisfiable if there is at least one interpretation (domain + assignment) that makes it true
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a formula CAN be made true by some model

terms.means: interpretation: a domain D plus assignment of predicates/constants

formulas.trap: Satisfiable does NOT mean valid — a formula can be true in some models and false in others.

formulas.name: Validity (Tautology in FOL)
formulas.plain: A FOL formula is valid if it is true in EVERY possible interpretation
formulas.whenToUse: Determining if a formula is logically true regardless of what the domain and predicates are
formulas.trap: FOL validity is undecidable (semi-decidable at best) — unlike propositional tautology which is decidable.

formulas.name: Finite vs. Infinite Models
formulas.latex: \text{If } \phi \text{ has only infinite models, then } \phi \text{ has no finite models (and vice versa possible)}
formulas.plain: Some FOL formulas are satisfied only by infinite domains; others only by finite ones; some by both or neither
formulas.whenToUse: Answering 'does this formula have a finite/infinite model?' type questions
formulas.trap: An anti-reflexive strict order (R(x,y) with irreflexivity + transitivity) has NO finite model because any finite chain must cycle — it needs an infinite chain.

formulas.name: Equivalence Relation in FOL (Reflexive + Symmetric + Transitive)
formulas.latex: \forall x\,R(x,x) \;\land\; \forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x)) \;\land\; \forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\land R(y,z)\to R(x,z))
formulas.plain: Reflexivity AND Symmetry AND Transitivity — together these define an equivalence relation
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing or checking whether a binary predicate R defines an equivalence relation in FOL
formulas.trap: The GATE question with anti-reflexivity (\forall x\,\neg R(x,x)) + symmetry + transitivity — this set has NO finite model (produces an infinite non-reflexive order or is unsatisfiable on finite sets).

formulas.name: Prenex Normal Form (PNF)
formulas.latex: Q_1 x_1\, Q_2 x_2 \cdots Q_n x_n\, \phi(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \quad Q_i \in \{\forall, \exists\}
formulas.plain: PNF: all quantifiers pulled to the front in a prefix, followed by a quantifier-free matrix
formulas.whenToUse: Converting an arbitrary FOL formula into an equivalent prenex form for resolution or proofs
formulas.trap: When moving quantifiers out of implication (A\to B), the quantifier in A flips type (\forall becomes \exists and vice versa) because A is in a negative position.

formulas.name: Substitution (Universal Instantiation)
formulas.latex: \frac{\forall x\, P(x)}{P(t)} \quad \text{(t is any term, free for x in P)}
formulas.plain: From 'for all x P(x)', infer P(t) for any term t — universal instantiation inference rule
formulas.whenToUse: Instantiating a universally quantified formula with a specific term during a proof

terms.means: a term (constant, variable, or function) substituted for x
substitution does not cause variable capture

formulas.trap: Variable capture — substituting t=y into \forall x\,\exists y\,P(x,y) without renaming y first gives a wrong result.

formulas.name: Existential Generalization
formulas.plain: If P(t) is true for some specific term t, then 'there exists x such that P(x)' is true
formulas.whenToUse: Introducing an existential quantifier in a proof when a witness term is available

terms.means: predicate P holds for the specific term t

formulas.trap: Existential generalization is always sound; confusing direction — you cannot universally generalize from a specific instance.

formulas.name: FOL Validity is Undecidable (Church's Theorem)
formulas.latex: \text{FOL validity is undecidable: no algorithm decides } \models \phi \text{ for all } \phi
formulas.plain: There is no algorithm that always halts and correctly decides whether a FOL sentence is valid
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about decidability of FOL satisfiability or validity

terms.means: phi is logically valid (true in all interpretations)

formulas.trap: FOL is semi-decidable (if valid, a proof procedure will eventually find it), but not decidable — there is no algorithm for the NO-instances.

formulas.name: FOL vs. Propositional Logic Expressiveness
formulas.plain: Propositional Logic is strictly less expressive than First-Order Logic, which is less expressive than Second-Order Logic
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing expressive power: what can/cannot be expressed in FOL that PL cannot
formulas.trap: FOL cannot express 'there are infinitely many x such that P(x)' as a single first-order sentence — that needs second-order or an infinite set of sentences.
